title: Run a Stage 5 Science Fair
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 5 Science to run a science fair that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage through real investigations.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the science from Stage 5 to run an accurate, safe and exciting science fair for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create a science-fair plan with a live investigation, labelled displays, a safety checklist, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your scientific decisions.

stages.title: 1. Foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Units 1, 2. Build a display that teaches each idea with a model or diagram, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Live investigation
stages.prompt: Pick one investigation from Units 3, 4 and run it live: state your hypothesis, follow the method, record results and explain your conclusion.
stages.evidence: A completed investigation with hypothesis, method, results and conclusion

stages.title: 3. Connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links ideas from Units 5, 6 to everyday life, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links to daily life

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your science fair. Explain at least three scientific choices, check that your conclusions match your evidence and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded scientific explanation

evidenceChecklist: A safety checklist for every activity
A connections wall with three links
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Observations, measurements, models and conclusions are accurate and checked.
Hypotheses, fair testing, recording and conclusions follow the scientific method.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the science visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate science words and evidence.

questions.question: Which part of a seed is the tiny baby plant?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: embryo The embryo is the tiny baby plant; the other parts protect or feed it.

questions.question: What is the main job of the seed coat?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: to protect the inside The tough seed coat guards the embryo and food store.

questions.question: Which part of a flower produces pollen?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: anther. The anther is the pollen-making tip of the stamen.

questions.question: The main job of colourful petals is to:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: attract pollinators. Bright petals advertise the flower to bees, butterflies and birds.

questions.question: Which state of matter keeps both a definite shape and a definite volume?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: solid. Only solids keep both a fixed shape and a fixed volume because their particles are locked in place.

questions.question: In which state are the particles far apart and moving quickly in all directions?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: gas. Gas particles are spread far apart and move quickly in every direction.

questions.question: Which of these is a light source?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a candle flame A candle makes its own light; the others only reflect.

questions.question: Light travels in:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: straight lines This is a basic property of light.

questions.question: A shadow forms when light is blocked by:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an opaque object. Only opaque materials block light fully to make a clear shadow; transparent things let light pass.

questions.question: The three things needed for a shadow are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: light, object, surface. All three must be present at once, or no shadow appears.

questions.question: What is at the centre of our Solar System?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Sun. The Sun sits at the centre and everything else orbits it.

questions.question: How long does Earth take to spin around once?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 24 hours. One full spin (rotation) takes 24 hours, which is why a day is 24 hours.
